data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-15T01:39:06.867Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about migrating Thread.Abort usage in their code. Let me look at the code they provided - it actually doesn't use Thread.Abort at all. It uses Thread.Start, Thread.Join, and Thread.Sleep, which all work fine in .NET 8.

However, they asked me to check the code and provide Thread.Abort migration guidance. I have a `thread-abort-migration` skill available that's specifically for this purpose. Let me invoke it.

Wait, let me re-read the skill description: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."


So the skill explicitly says not to use it for this case. The code doesn't actually use Thread.Abort, Thread.Interrupt, or catch ThreadAbortException, so I should just tell the user directly that their code doesn't need any migration for those APIs. I can still offer suggestions about modernizing to Task-based patterns, but the specialized migration skill isn't the right tool here.
